Back: Categories used to analyse language including lexis semantics grammar phonology discourse and pragmatics.
The vocabulary used in a text.
The study of meaning in language.
The system of rules governing how words combine to form sentences.
The arrangement of words and phrases to form sentences.
The study of word structure and formation.
The study of sound patterns in spoken language.
The visual features of written language such as punctuation layout and font.
Language used in communication beyond the sentence level.
The study of how context influences meaning.
The smallest unit of meaning in language.
A morpheme that can stand alone as a word.
A morpheme that must attach to another word such as prefixes or suffixes.
Changing a word to show tense number or comparison.
Creating new words by adding prefixes or suffixes.
A word that names a person place thing or concept.
A word that expresses an action state or occurrence.
A word that describes a noun.
A word that modifies a verb adjective or another adverb.
A word placed before a noun to clarify meaning such as the or a.
A word used instead of a noun.
A word showing relationships of place time or direction.
A word used to connect clauses or sentences.
A group of words containing a subject and a verb.
A group of words that function together but do not contain both subject and verb.
A sentence containing one independent clause.
A sentence with two main clauses joined by a conjunction.
A sentence containing a main clause and at least one subordinate clause.
The grammatical and lexical links that connect ideas in a text.
The logical flow and clarity of a text.
A group of words related in meaning.
A comparison where something is described as something else.
A comparison using like or as.
Language that creates vivid sensory descriptions.
A word or phrase used to organise speech such as well or so.
Words whose meaning depends on context such as here now this or that.
Language expressing possibility obligation or certainty.
Language used to soften statements or reduce certainty.
The level of formality used in language.
The intended receiver of a text.
The reason a text is created.
The situation in which communication takes place.
Making certain language features stand out for emphasis.
Repeating words or structures for emphasis.
Using similar grammatical structures for effect.
The omission of words that are understood from context.
A sentence used to ask a question.
A sentence used to give a command.
A sentence used to make a statement.
A sentence expressing strong emotion.
The detailed examination of language features to interpret meaning.
